seo.title: Building Web Analytics with open-source tools
seo.description: A step-by-step implementation guide for Web Analytics with open-source tools.

content.intro: This guide outlines the implementation of a privacy-first, cookie-less web analytics strategy. It focuses on bypassing ad-blockers via reverse proxying and instrumenting custom event tracking for product-led growth metrics without requiring user consent banners.
prerequisites: A web application (React, Next.js, or Vue preferred)
A privacy-first analytics provider account (Plausible, Umami, or Fathom)
Access to DNS settings or a reverse proxy like Nginx/Vercel Rewrites

steps.title: Configure a Reverse Proxy for the Tracking Script
steps.explanation: To prevent ad-blockers from stripping your analytics script, proxy the tracking script through your own domain. This ensures the request originates from your first-party domain rather than a known tracking CDN.
pitfalls: Failure to proxy the API endpoint (/api/event) will result in script loading but data failing to send.
Ensure your analytics provider supports custom domains or proxying before implementation.

steps.title: Initialize the Tracking Snippet
steps.explanation: Inject the script into your application's head. Use data-attributes to define the domain and ensure the script points to your proxied path rather than the provider's default URL.
pitfalls: Setting data-domain incorrectly will lead to a 403 Forbidden error from the analytics collector.
Loading the script synchronously will negatively impact Core Web Vitals (LCP).

steps.title: Implement Custom Event Tracking for UI Interactions
steps.explanation: Standard pageview tracking is insufficient for modern apps. Use a global helper to trigger custom events for specific user actions like 'Signup Clicked' or 'AI Prompt Submitted'.
pitfalls: Do not include Personally Identifiable Information (PII) like email addresses in props to maintain GDPR compliance.
Ensure the script has finished loading before calling the tracking function, or use a queueing pattern.

steps.title: Track Server-Side Events for AI Features
steps.explanation: Client-side tracking can miss events if a user closes the tab before an AI response finishes. Trigger a server-side POST request to the analytics API when an AI generation completes.
pitfalls: Server-side calls require manual User-Agent and X-Forwarded-For headers to accurately record geographic data.
Rate limits on the analytics provider's API can drop server-side events if not handled with a retry logic.

steps.title: Verify Data Accuracy and Payload Structure
steps.explanation: Open the browser's Network tab and filter by the proxied path. Trigger an event and verify the payload is a valid JSON object with the expected properties. Check that no cookies are being set in the Response Headers.
pitfalls: Testing with an active ad-blocker that uses CNAME cloaking detection might still block the proxy; test in a clean environment.
Verify that the 'domain' field in the payload matches exactly what is configured in your analytics dashboard.

content.conclusion: You now have a robust, privacy-compliant analytics setup that avoids common ad-blocking pitfalls. By using a reverse proxy and custom event helpers, you can track deep product engagement metrics while maintaining a zero-cookie footprint.
